Axiom Verge es un metroidvania de lo más peculiar desarrollado en exclusiva por el multifacético Tom Happ. Y es muy meritorio habida cuenta de que a pesar de sus recursos limitados (es un indie de presupuesto modesto) propone una experiencia lúdica muy bien cohesionada artísticamente.   Desde sus primeros compases el juego evoca inevitablemente a mi Metroid, tanto en ambientación como en estructura. Su atmósfera opresiva, los entornos alienígenas y ese sentimiento de aislamiento que se transmite permanentemente son reminiscencias evidentes a la franquicia de culto de Nintendo, que considero superior a todos los niveles, obviamente.   Este “homenaje”, sin embargo, tiene una marcada personalidad propia y de hecho reinterpreta aquellas experiencias desde un enfoque más experimental y surrealista, incorporando “glitches” como parte del diseño y una narrativa que focaliza su discurso en el conflicto entre determinismo y libertad y que se adentra en lo abstracto y lo metafísico aún a riesgo de ser más críptica de lo necesario.  Técnicamente no tengo nada que objetar. El juego homenajea la estética retro pero con una cierta complejidad visual muy agradecida, especialmente en los efectos de distorsión,  las ráfagas de las numerosísimas armas, los jefes de fase y las localizaciones “corrompidas” por fallos digitales y glitches. Visualmente, sabe hacerse original. La OST mezcla sintetizadores y melodías de punto inquietante que andamian esa atmósfera alienígena, opresiva y abstrusa.   En lo mecánico, nada fuera de lo ordinario a nivel de género con la excepción de los antedichos “glitches” y el terminal de códigos, que previo análisis de según qué documentos repartidos por los niveles nos permite alterar el escenario en zonas muy puntuales para franquear fases inaccesibles o zonas ocultas, añadiendo una capa de profundidad adicional que recompensa la experimentación.   Si acaso, como aspecto mejorable, esa mecánica no la introducen ni explican convincentemente y puede pasar bastante desapercibida, lo que impedirá acceder a algunas armas, módulos de salud o potenciadores, documentos específicos e incluso fases ocultas.  Porque sí, por más recomendable que me parezca el juego (y así lo considero) no está exento de críticas. El arsenal de armas es francamente extenso (puede rondar la veintena fácilmente) pero muchas de ellas resultan poco prácticas o redundantes lo que acaba desincentivando su uso, especialmente porque hay opciones letales “inobviables” (en mi caso, el lanzallamas devino en el arma definitiva en todo el último acto).  Por otra parte el desarrollo del juego, por momentos, llega a ser excesivamente críptico al punto de generar desconcierto porque no acaba de concretar en multitud de ocasiones hacia dónde avanzar con claridad. Consciente de ello, el desarrollador incluyó la posibilidad de colocar marcadores en el mapa pero de forma limitada (diría que se limitan a dos o quizás tres) pero nuevamente la introducción de esa mecánica es apenas anecdótica y puede pasar bastante desapercibida.   Para concluir, la historia intenta disertar sobre tropos sci-fi interesantes con tintes metafísicos y filosóficos pero resulta bastante enrevesada e incluso poco inspirada puntualmente.  Dicho lo cual, y en conjunto, Axiom Verge es un juego bastante recomendable a poco que vaya el género y deviene en una experiencia desafiante y original que merece reconocimiento.

7/10 玩起来感觉年轻了50岁，不似21世纪的人类了。完全体自机性能极强，但开局只有1%的实力，一路搜集新能力非常爽。随着自机越来越变态，敌人能力也越来越逆天，而不是单纯随玩家提升数值，每个新区域都很有意思。boss战都比较死板，站对位置选对武器就能乱杀，比堆叠一排从岩浆里跳出来释放超振声波的小怪房间舒适很多。部分小怪的处理非常墨迹，重复游玩体验较差。

Really nice metroidvania. There are two aspects that clearly stand out: - Exploration. It's really satisfying to find weapons, collectibles, power-ups and abilities around the map. Many of them require careful attention and are pretty well hidden, but the game will somehow tell you that there might be something there if you pay attention. This is really well done. - Mechanics. Every time I thought "I need a new ability here, like a double jump, a dash..." I was wrong. The mechanics are original and different from the usual ideas, and they combine perfectly with each other. Level design also helps to achieve this.

Had a good time with this game, I finished in fairly quickly, took me around 15 hours to complete it 100% and that felt like a good length to me. The visuals are awesome, love the variety in the environments/areas you visit, especially in terms of the colour palette that's presented to the player here. For me, and I imagine a lot of (if not most) of the people who love and play metroidvanias, how the controls feel is often a make or break thing and AV's controls feel great - just the right amount of responsiveness, no weird delay in the jumps, you're in complete control of the character and that often means you have to be precise in your movement in regards to traversal/movement/combat/etc, which I love too.  Really nice variety of weapons (there's like, a ♥♥♥♥ ton more than I expected there to be), at one point I had 5 or 6 and I assumed I had, at most, 2 weapons left to find, but turns out the weapon wheel expands a huge amount. This is nice because you can play around with a lot and it gives you different tools for different situations, but I did find that there were some weapons I ended up not really using just because some are way handier and more practical to use, or just deal more damage. Also has plenty of "tools" (this game's movement abilities/upgrades) which obviously is a must for this type of game, and it's very fun going back to certain areas to re-explore nooks and crannies you didn't have access to before. I was sometimes a bit confused as to where I was supposed to go to next, but admittedly it was often because I hadn't looked close enough or hadn't realised I could use a certain ability a certain way.  Nice amount of bosses, some I found more frustrating than fun due to some annoying mechanics they had, but I could just be really bad and was trying to brute force my way through it too much. The regular enemies are a nice challenge too, and I love that you can "glitch" the enemies to essentially give them debuffs and transform them, which sometimes can be used as an exploration tool. Super cool feature  Cool music too but that kindaaaa got on my nerves at times cause the tracks loop and if you're exploring one area a lot you're just hearing the same thing over and over again, and again that might be my bad, maybe I just had it on too loud. Kur especially I found grating lol but otherwise dope OST. So I enjoyed myself, and the story is intriguing and I definitely wanna watch a chunky lore video on this game cause the universe that Tom Happ has built is unique and clearly full of fun lore. Not sure how often I'd come back to this, probably not as regularly as I do with other MVs like HK, Blasphemous, Dead Cells, but definitely still recommend this to MV lovers. May pick up the sequel but the negative reviews aren't particularly enticing lol so idk about that yet

100%收集，很好玩的类银河城，通关流程较短，15h左右。  Boss房附近都有存档点+明显提示下一间是boss房，好文明！战斗难度不高，基本就是躲弹幕、攻击弱点，掌握节奏就很好打。 可惜没有传送，好在地图也不大，中央有一条“交通”道连接，乘坐一个神秘工具头就能抵达各个区域，也算是一种稍微麻烦的“传送”吧。  能力锁方面最有意思的是Hack枪和小机器兵的设计。Hack枪对不同敌人都有不同效果：干扰、化敌为友、心灵操控[strike]（尤里：兄弟，想控你.jpg）[/strike]、切图后复制敌人位置等等， 探图时候多对敌人使用黑客技能会有惊喜。 小机器兵钻洞简直是探图小能手，后期解锁交换以后更是让主角位移能力大幅提升。  缺点是钩爪的使用比较便秘，摇摆不能提供动量很奇怪，发射时候还带伸缩时间，比较劝退，在后期拿到三级衣服和机器兵传送后就基本宣告退休了。 游戏内有的文件内容有些是乱码文怪唬人的，其实是有两套语言Sudran和Rusalki，拿到相关文件按内容直接输密码就能解开。另外地图上有四处地方需要用密码开图，在附近的环境或敌人身上有提示，除了有一个在文件内容里，可以获得一把神器喷火枪。  总之，很推荐，适合喜欢探索地图的类银爱好者

It is a real interesting game in terms of the world and lore associated with it. As a Metroidvania game, it is more to the style of Metroid than the vania association. Traversal is not that great as you don't have very many ways of getting to either side of the game-world besides a ridiculous tram thing. The map isn't so huge that you will hate the multiple corridors you can pass through to get to the place you want to go but if you get stuck you might spend some time dealing with repeated enemy encounters.   The mixture of enemies are very much Metroid inspired with lots of awkward moving ones that you can only deal with by pumping them full of whatever bullet type you wish to choose. There are way more kinds of bullets than is ever needed in this game which is fun for variety if you want to do multiple runs of the game. I only did 1 playthrough of this game so it didn't so it stuck out to me and made me feel a little disappointed that there wasn't some kind of combination of bullet types like Metroid.   The bosses are all really good but there is a lack of significantly challenging fights. You find a good place to stand and just blast away and everything eventually falls to the ground.   Its a fun game and well worth playing.

Axiom Verge est bien plus qu’un clone de Metroid. C’est un excellent jeu de plateforme et d’action-aventure où l’esthétique 8-bit est sublimée : ambiance NES, nostalgie à la clé — une vraie madeleine de Proust. Les graphismes sont soignés et la bande-son, avec sa touche de modernité, fait merveille.
Es una grandiosa aventura, llena de secretos, transformaciones y excelente desenlace.
Jogo muito bom, com uma historia bem envolvente, ele tem muita coisa dos Metroids antigos. Recomendo muito.

Mal équilibré et frustrant malgré un bon potentiel  J’avais envie d’aimer Axiom Verge. L’univers et la direction artistique sont intéressants, mais le jeu souffre de gros problèmes de design et d’équilibrage. La progression est souvent confuse : on se perd facilement non pas parce que c’est difficile, mais parce que le level design manque de clarté. Beaucoup de zones se ressemblent, les allers-retours sont forcés, et certaines armes ou compétences arrivent trop tard pour être réellement utiles.  Le gameplay lui-même devient vite frustrant — certains ennemis sont soit trop faibles, soit ridiculement punitifs, et les boss ne donnent pas toujours une vraie sensation de progression. On sent qu’il y avait de bonnes idées, mais elles sont mal exploitées.  En résumé : un metroidvania qui mise sur la nostalgie sans offrir la finesse ou la cohérence qu’on attend du genre. Dommage, car le potentiel était bien là.

An excellent Metroid styled platform-shooter with tight controls, great soundtrack and thought-provoking story. The story might be a bit too open-ended and leave too much open to interpretation for my taste, but is interesting enough to keep you digging for more clues and details throughout the game.

Axiom Verge es básicamente un fangame de Super Metroid. Exploré una gran parte del mapa (98%) y obtuve la mayoría de los items (84%, muchos siendo simplemente notas que solo aportan lore), y siento que es un juego que siendo más chico sería mucho mejor (véase Gato Roboto). Muchas de las mejores las sentí innecesarias, y muchas armas fueron extremadamente circunstanciales o directamente inservibles. Los controles los sentía bastante bien hasta que introdujeron ciertas mecánicas que lo hicieron frustrante, sobre todo el doble tap (en joystick, con el analógico o el d-pad) que tenés que hacer para el dash, frustración fácilmente evitable con una opción de remappear los controles que el juego no provee. Hay enemigos que son un dolor de pelotas de lidiar justamente por esto. Los jefes al principio zafan pero en el late-game se vuelven bastante mediocres y hasta brillan por su ausencia. Por último, la historia no es nada del otro mundo y recién se vuelve interesante sobre la mitad del juego, donde todo comienza a esclarecerse.  A pesar de todo esto, hay muchas cosas buenas sobre este juego: una es el pixel-art hermoso que tiene, y la atmósfera que genera su mundo y la música; algo que Super Metroid logra muchísimo mejor, pero tampoco vamos a comparar un indie con un clásico histórico. Los diseños y los ataques de los enemigos son en general variados, y el mundo es divertido de explorar. Lo único que le falta en este sentido es un fast-travel real, ya que a veces se hace un poco tedioso el backtracking.  En general, me tiro más para el sí que para el no. Si te gusta Super Metroid en específico este no te va a desagradar, pero tampoco te va a cambiar la vida. Simplemente va a ser un juego más que hayas jugado. Si estás buscando algo épico como Hollow Knight te diría que lo busques en otro lado. Este juego no tiene ni su narrativa ni sus jefes.

Here's a quick review of my thoughts after beating "Axiom Verge" on the PC.   + Graphics are a nice pixel art style. Environments look good and all the sprite work is well done. + Upgrades are great. A lot of the later ones you get really open up the map for you. Makes you go, “Oh, now I can finally do this and that!” They are also pretty fun to use. +- Story isn’t too bad but pretty unengaging. I was constantly forgetting character names and plot points. Literally, it would've been ten times easier to understand what was going on if they displayed the character name of who is talking in the speech bubbles. +- Progression is a mix between very smooth and painstakingly cryptic. It can be very hard to pinpoint where exactly you need to go next. I’ll admit I had to look at a walkthrough a couple times because I was completely and utterly stumped as to what to do. This game reminds me a lot of the original “Metroid” with the huge amount of “Where do I go? What do I do?” moments it gave me. +- The map is basic. Main downsides are that it’s not too detailed and you can only set 2 markers on it. Why only 2? This game doesn’t mark any points of interest itself and you can’t set a marker to look a certain way to remind yourself if you need a specific item to progress there. Dumb. +- Enemy design is pretty good. A couple later ones are insane to deal with though. I always ended up using cheap tactics to deal with them or just used the drone to avoid them entirely. +- Bosses are a pretty good fair difficulty. There was one though that straight up backed me into a corner and then flamethrowered me. That was fun… +- Controls are okay. Walking and shooting feel fine but the grapple hook you get is a pain to deal with. Plus, I wish the dash wasn’t assigned to double pressing the D-pad/Joystick. I also wish they made it clearer that you could dash both up and down. I had no idea you could do that. - Music is pretty bad. I had to actually turn it off in the last two hours of my playthrough since it was just unsufferable to listen to. Like, most 8-bit music from random NES games are better than this soundtrack - Why is there no fast travel? Getting from one area of this game to the next is a nightmare. Yes, there is a middle section of the map that does have some sort of “fast travel” system, but it is still so slow. Plus, there are so many times in this game where you get an upgrade in one area and only actually use it to progress on the complete opposite side of the map.   Overall quality of experience: 7/10. I Didn’t Like this game. To me… do I end a lot of reviews with “To me”? Idaknow. Anyone, to me, this felt like if the original Metroid got ported to the Sega Genesis and they just added a map and save feature. It is that underwhelming and downright boring and annoying at times. I had to practically force myself to finish this game, it is not a good Metroidvania. It lacks all the quality of life features that freakin’ “Super Metroid” introduced back in 1994. I mean, even “Symphony of the Night” had fast travel. Overall… well, I do totally end a lot of my reviews with “Overall”. Um, anyway, yeah, don’t play this game. It is the most barebones Metroidvania I’ve ever seen from an indie developer. Literally, play anything else.

Axiom Verge es una joya absoluta del género metroidvania y uno de esos juegos que te recuerdan por qué te enamoraste de los videojuegos en primer lugar. Todo en él está cuidado al detalle: desde su ambientación retro que homenajea a los clásicos de 8 y 16 bits, hasta la cantidad de secretos y sorpresas que esconde en cada rincón.  Lo primero que llama la atención es su atmósfera. Cada zona tiene un estilo propio, con una estética pixel art impecable y un diseño sonoro que consigue envolverte en un mundo extraño, alienígena y perturbador. La música acompaña de maravilla: hipnótica, inquietante y a la vez motivadora, perfecta para perderte durante horas explorando.  La jugabilidad es otro de sus puntos fuertes. El juego ofrece un arsenal enorme de armas y habilidades, cada una con su función y su personalidad. Lo más impresionante es cómo consigue que todas resulten útiles en algún momento, invitándote a experimentar y a encontrar nuevas formas de avanzar. La sensación de descubrimiento es constante: siempre hay un pasillo oculto, un secreto escondido o un jefe que te pondrá a prueba.  Lo que realmente destaca es el mimo con el que está hecho. Un solo desarrollador logró crear un mundo enorme, cohesivo y lleno de detalles que rivaliza con sagas míticas. Se nota la pasión y el respeto hacia los clásicos, pero sin quedarse en la nostalgia: Axiom Verge tiene identidad propia, con mecánicas únicas como el “glitch gun”, que cambian por completo la manera de interactuar con los enemigos y el entorno.  En resumen, Axiom Verge no solo es un homenaje a los metroidvania, es uno de los mejores exponentes modernos del género. Si te gusta explorar, descubrir y sentir que cada nueva habilidad abre puertas a un universo más grande, este juego es imprescindible. Una obra maestra indie que se disfruta de principio a fin.

Axiom Verge was a disappointment. Widely claimed as a great Metroidvania, which is my favorite genre, I assumed there would be no way not to enjoy this game, right? But it fails at the three things that makes Metroidvanias incredible for me - combat, movement, and exploration.  Starting with combat. Axiom Verge gives you one option: ranged combat. You're stuck to the 8-way directional for aiming your shots, and while you have a huge range of weapons (I beat the game with 12 or so), most are useless or incredibly situational. You'll frequently be unable to hit enemies a bit above or below you because of the odd angles the 8-way directional forces on you, and while I can see this being an aesthetic design choice ("Look, we're retro, let's emulate Metroid"), I'm sure retro games would have preferred free aiming if they had the tech to do so at the time. Axiom Verge does, decides to not use it, and it's a lesser game for it.  Now, movement. It starts bad, like any other Metroidvania, but barely improves. There's no running, double jumping, wall climbing or hanging, dash, or any of the actual cool movement abilities you would expect. Instead, you get a janky-as-hell grappling hook that barely functions, yet is required to progress into many frustrating areas of the game; you get a teleport that also decides to work whenever it wants, not when you want it to; and finally, a spider drone, that half the time fails to launch and your character launches the hook instead. Not only is mobility limited, the limited mobility you have is also bad. Considering the absurd amount of backtracking and verticality on display here, it's wild they decide this was good enough. With poor combat and poor movement, you cannot create memorable boss fights or gauntlets, so the game doesn't have the same oomph that the Mantis Lords in Hollow Knight, Elsom the Oger in Ghost Song, or Granfaloon in Castlevania SOTN.  And finally, exploration. I didn't mind the lore at first, but it mostly went nowhere. I think my biggest issue here is that the world is uninteresting - it's very samey and barren. There are little to no NPC's, only the giant sleeping heads telepathically talking to you that I found very hard to empathize. You hear of the villain but don't see it or it's actions until the last 3 minutes of the game. There is little to interact with, no one to talk to, and all areas are kind of generic and similar, so it feels you're going around in circles while playing the game.  Sincerely, I have a very high bar for Metroidvanias, and Axiom Verge is far from what I would consider a good one. At least I also got Blasphemous, so let's see what it is all about shall we

The year is 2017. A friend of mine sends me 2 pirated games saying "you should try them both, I know you love metroidvanias".  One is a game called "Hollow Knight". I play it for a few hours. Looks nice, but it really doesn't catch my attention. The other one is called "Axiom Verge": same, never heard of it. I launch Axiom Verge. I meet a guy named Trace. As time goes on, a stream of dopamine invades me and goes through my body.  My family calls me to have dinner with them. I look at the time: it's been 3 hours. I decide to call it a day. The next morning, I wake up at 6 a.m, turn on my PC and launch Axiom Verge. The data files are gone, the game is gone as well.  The year is 2025. This game pops up in my Steam's recommended list. I buy it, download it and launch it.   "Sorry Trace, I'm late."

I've played through this game a number of times.  It is the best metroidvania experience in decades, up there and I feel in many ways surpassing Super Metroid and Castlevania Sotn.  There is a deep layered story full of mystery, and to tell ahead, the sequel tries to do some things that arent as polished and fun, but is worth playing for getting a bigger picture of the story, because there are so many frayed ends your missing, but its beautiful, fun, challenging, will throw you for a loop multiple times, this game is the high bar, and i think it destroys the modern metroidvanias.  Matrix meets Metroid is def the energy here, if you haven't played this yet and are considering it, jump in, there are a few puzzles you may need to look up to find everything, but most can be found combing through the info you've been given ingame, but even if not your thing, it just hides a few items, you'll still have a good time.

Axiom Verge was surprisingly good. I picked it up on a deep discount thinking it would be a throw away game to kill a few hours on. However, it turned out to be really solid. It is a Metroid leaning Metroidvania and follows the typical formula of wander the map, get stuck, fight a boss, get a movement based upgrade to get past the obstacle, repeat. What this game does really well is atmosphere and gameplay polish. The music and graphics do well to give a gloomy otherworldly feel, while the controls feel intuitive and responsive. If you are a fan of the genre, this one is worth picking up.

I give it a "maybe". This game is not for everyone, especially if you are used to modern metroidvanias that mix Soulslike gameplay with lighter metroidvania exploration (and a lot of QoL).  This game definitely draws inspiration from earlier metroidvanias. If you are aiming for a completionist run, prepare plenty of paper so you can note and map out your discoveries, or follow a walkthrough. There are no tools to help you seek what you might have missed, and exploration is very slow, with limited quick travel methods.  But if you are simply on the lookout for a unique world to explore, it is a very unique game, with an amazing soundtrack. Just enjoy and do not worry about missing out a few upgrades or weapons!

我挺能理解这个游戏无论是怪物设计还是地图配色都想要给玩家营造出一种诡异荒诞的氛围，但至少就我而言我对此是真的无动于衷啊。  最后评价： 说实话我玩过的游戏中很少有那种会给我一种不明所以的纳闷儿的感觉，但是这个游戏做到了。  从前两张地图毫无感觉的boss战反馈和进入第三张地图后显著堆怪情况出现的开始，我就应该早点意识到这就是一个很一般甚至不及格的作品，我完全不能理解为什么人物性能发展到了第三张地图在至少拥有了5种武器的情况下却仍然对满地图堆积的怪物缺乏有效的反制手段，对此我就只能解释为制作组纯粹的恶意或是无能了。  虽然我看了评价反馈得知其是由作者独自开发四年推出的作品，但仍给我一种闭门造车且粗制滥造的感觉，所谓的吹捧究竟是何意啊。  我认为没有必要在这一系列的作品上浪费时间，除非是在实在没有其他选择的情况下。

The only metroidvania better than Axiom Verge is Super Metroid. Somehow one lone developer has nailed the perfect level design, creature design, boss encounters and player movement but then took it further with a vast array of weapons, dozens of secrets, unique "address corruptor" mechanics, [i]stunning[/i] original music and deeply compelling sci-fi lore in the vein of PC classics like Another World.   Axiom Verge isn't a game, it's an [i]experience[/i]. Buy it. Buy it NOW.   (PS: The sequel is different but equally fabulous)

I had high hopes for this game, the art alone had me frothing at the mouth to learn the lore, explore the world, and fight geiger-esque monstrosities.  Unfortunately what i got was pretty uninspired and clunky: bad map system, wild goose chases for useless weapon upgrades, EXTREMELY bare bones and terribly paced story, boring/badly designed enemies and bosses, and worst of all, a world that feels BAD to traverse even with all the upgrades.  The map: inacurate as HELL when it comes to bigger areas: will have you running around trying to fill out a single square that's probably behind a wall two levels above you when according to the map it's right in front of you.  The weapon upgrades: most of these will get you killed if you try to use them and are annoying to acquire, i should never be trying to proceed with the story only to find a secret item and be disappointed instead of pleasantly surprised.  The story: in my opinion it's pretty bad. It strings you along with a dialogue box or two every six hours and gives you almost nothing. No lore on the enemies, the areas, or the items.  Every boss provides ZERO exposition and has the same dialogue, so why even have them speak? There's notes scattered around that provide nothing interesting, or are in an alien language that you can't even read. As interesting as the areas LOOK, the environmental storytelling here is pretty nonexistent.   The bosses/enemies: SPONGY and erratic, and more than willing to take cheap shots.  Nothing like being BUSHWHACKED by a turbo speeding/falling alien while just trying to make it to the next save point with zero health. This game is by no means difficult but it's definitely tedious to shoot the same enemy 63 times when you've memorized his pattern and he poses no threat to you, but if you try to walk by he WILL hit you. If you've played this game and you disagree, think back to the flying circular robots with dangling tails.  The bosses are almost not worth mentioning because they're all exactly the same. They're all the same guy and have the same dialogue. Half the fun in a metroidvania is uncovering the horrors that the planet/area holds: monsters, demons, the undead, experiments gone wrong, body horror aliens etc. No such fun here, once you've seen the first boss you've kinda seen them all.  There's two exceptions i can think of; a giant bug (hell yeah) and the final boss.  The traversal: The glitch gun can get you stuck WAY too often holy hell. i had to restart my game MULTIPLE TIMES because i was stuck in a 1X2  glitch zone and couldn't get out. If the thing just cleared all the glitch away quickly and cleanly, it wouldn't be an issue, but the thing is so inconsistent that it's visually ambiguous where you could to a teleport or something, The controls/limitations on the teleport thing is just awful and frustrating. You WILL spend time mashing directions on the dpad because you're not sure if the teleport is just being stupid, or if the wall/ceiling actually can't be bypassed. Stacking the drone launch and the teleport together should feel satisfying and slick like Mario's hat throw in odyssey, instead it just feels like trying to do a special move on an MK2 cabinet that hasn't been repaired since 1997. I'm sure there's plenty of areas that i missed because i didn't want to spend 30 minutes mashing out an awkward frame perfect teleport/drone combo.  I typically ADORE games like this (metroid, castlevania, tails of iron, blasphemous, etc etc) but this one had me FIENDING for that bland ending. The art on the Rusalki is haunting and gorgeous, it's a shame the game doesn't deliver on that vibe anywhere else in the game.   I CAN however recommend buying the OST, some bangin' chiptunes here.

Um excelente jogo do gênero Metroidvania em que embarcamos numa aventura com Trace pelo mundo de Sudra. O jogo toca em temas como viagem no tempo, multiverso, os perigos da IA e múltiplas personalidades. Adorei a exploração, as armas e as habilidades, os chefes, a trilha sonora e a história. Vale muito a pena ler as notas que contam um pouco da história de Sudra e tentam desvendar o mistério por trás da presença de Trace nesse mundo em ruínas.

Came into this game not really expecting much, but it came out as one of my favorite games I've ever played. Dont think I've ever played anything else where after beating the game I wanted to do the Speedrun achievs.  I dont really have too much expierience with Metroid type games, I usually play stuff more similar to Castlevania in the Metroidvania genre, but this game made me more interested in games similar to Metroid. Although one thing in this game that I could see being a bit polarizing is the aiming mechanics, as those are similar to Cuphead which might take a bit getting used to.  I wouldnt recommend going for 100% of stuff in this game, for me this game thrives on trying to go as minimal in resources as possible, and a lot of the secrets are kinda eh, where I cant even see how you could discover them without some external help.  The game has a built-in randomizer with different modifiers, which I feel like is a great way of extending gameplay if someone wants something fresh after beating the game a couple of times.  I like the power ups in this game, they're pretty unique with some allowing you to change how enemies act, meanwhile another one allows you to literally walk through walls, which I dont think I've ever seen something similar to that in other games. I also like how a lot of the bosses can be countered with weapons that could be unlocked right before them, so the game really rewards the player for either trying to explore or just having knowledge about the game.  Would 100% recommend playing it if you like Metroidvania games.

a love letter to metroid that doesn't copy its homework. lovely atmospheric soundtrack (third(?) area is a standout), and the way the beat of each track is incorporated into the graphics and the low-health beeps is really nice. visuals and sfx on attacks/tool use are incredibly satisfying.   just play it. if you're looking at these reviews it's because you've played metroid and probably liked it, and you're wondering if you'll like this too. yes. yes you will.

It's always impressive when a decade old game can still bring a ton of surprises. Despite the genre having more and more groundbreaking titles, Axiom Verge managed to still give me a unique experience unlike all the rest. I would definitely say it fits the "metroid" of metroidvania more, but even in a lot of metroid games I don't feel the sense of discovery that axiom verge gave. Every corner I found a new upgrade or weapon that I had never seen before and it's genuinely impressive. I don't think the other weapons are all great, some felt pretty useless, but just how unique they are combined with the satisfaction of finding them is enough. I think early on it can be tough fighting bosses with the little health you have, but it's pretty quick to get back to them usually. Easily still manages to be one of the best metroidvanias.

played this mostly on Wii U (gasp!) through unofficial means. then i heard about the man himself and mentally tugged my collar homer simpson style.  he has a great name btw.  metroidvania that is more metroid than vania, massively inspired by the NES original and H.R. Giger's works, with cool story themes, and cool music. doesn't feel like anus to play like most NES games.  EDIT: 7/3/24: One of the parts I love about the gameplay is the addition of a drill, a high-damage close-range 'melee' style ability. If you can get in close and hold down the trigger you can take care of some more annoying enemies.
能力设计放到现在都不过时，转化敌人更是能玩出很多花样。没有刁难人的跳跳乐和boss，对手残党非常友好。存档点密度和分布都很合理，不搜索隐藏的话不用担心365里路，要是有传送就更好了。

I liked it for the most part, it starts well, but when most of the map is open it becomes kind of confusing what to do and where to go next... in other words: I spent a lot of time lost and stuck not knowing what to do. I resorted to watching a walkthrough up to the point where I could find what I missed because going back and forth through the map aimlessly gets tiring after a few days (I got a lot of weapons and power-ups because of this, though).  The music is very good, graphics are nice (although I prefer 16-bit over 8-bit pixel graphics, this is kind of in the middle), the controls are generally OK but it depends on what you're trying to do or what you're using (the grapple is not very good, for example), the atmosphere is excellent, the story I could do without (seriously, it'd probably better if the game just didn't explain anything except for some clues here and there) and the dialogue is cringey / high school homework level (which is probably OK for the type of game this tries to be, but still).  Combat is kind of ♥♥♥♥, I think. Enemies are either just there or super difficult. Same for the bosses: the first 4 or 5 bosses where super easy, and then you got some mega difficulty spike with some kind of impossible hornet that took watching another video and many many MANY tries. Then you have another 2 or 3 bosses that I don't think fit the tools at your disposal, don't telegraph their movements very well, and... I don't know. They're not very well designed, I think. Also the gauntlets before those last few bosses can be quite difficult, as opposed to the rest of the game.  Anyways, I'm not very fond of "the old times" nor of nostalgia-ware but it was OK, I think. Even if I'd never finish a game like this in the old days (or in the present day without some external help). I just wish I could like it more.

Обычно в подобных играх ты ждешь что новые апгреды упрощают тебе прохождение, однако тут они наоборот - ломаю управление, все усложняют, делают игру невероятно неуклюжей. А без них пройти - никак. Плюс еще бой крайне посредственный и ухудшается теми же проблемами. А ведь визуальный стиль, дизайн уровней и сюжет - действительно хороши и интересны, но геймплей напрочь убивает желание проходить игру полностью.

This game is immaculate and brought me back to the days where I played Metroid 1, and Super on Dad's consoles. I think it's really intelligently designed in how the game wordlessly encourages you to experiment. Combat against smaller enemies usually gives you plenty of options. Learning not only how the creature behaves, but how the hacking mechanic shifts what each enemy can do is such a rewarding experience. Bosses tend to have pretty simple patterns, but gradually get faster as they lose health. This is both a positive and a negative, since easy patterns can become fairly tight to dodge through, and already difficult patterns can spiral out of control.   There's a large variety of weapons that all seem to have strengths and weaknesses for every sort of baddie you'd encounter. But in that, the game does get a bit frustrating near the end. Flying enemies attacking you on sight, tanky enemies shooting you from afar... in one case, Both cases are true. But you can find and make creative solutions to all of them.  Axiom Verge is a creative masterpiece, that has a few flaws that didn't detract from my experience. It knows what it wants to be, and it does it all really well. And the native randomizer ensures that your second playthrough can bring back that same sense of curiosity you had during your first run.  Bottled nostalgia mixed with a mind ♥♥♥♥. 8/10

Axiom Verge is a remarkable homage to classic Metroidvania-style games, yet it distinguishes itself through a unique blend of retro aesthetics, intricate design, and a deeply immersive sci-fi narrative. Developed by Thomas Happ Games, this indie title first captured the attention of gamers with its pixel-perfect art style and fluid gameplay, showcasing the potential of one-person development projects. At its core, Axiom Verge offers a sprawling, interconnected world filled with secret passages, mysterious technology, and challenging enemies, encouraging exploration and rewarding curiosity with a constant sense of discovery.  The game’s visuals are a loving nod to the 16-bit era, with detailed pixel art that feels both nostalgic and fresh. Environments range from alien caverns to eerie laboratories, each area meticulously crafted to evoke a haunting atmosphere that complements the storyline. The sound design deserves equal praise; the haunting synth-heavy soundtrack perfectly underscores the mood, immersing players in a mysterious world that feels both expansive and intimate. Audio cues are cleverly used to enhance gameplay, from the ominous hum of machinery to the subtle beeps signaling the discovery of hidden upgrades.  Gameplay in Axiom Verge revolves around exploring the vast map, acquiring new weapons and abilities, and solving environmental puzzles. The controls are tight and responsive, allowing for smooth platforming and intense combat encounters. One of the game’s standout features is its arsenal of inventive weapons, each with unique mechanics that encourage creative approaches to combat and exploration. The ability to switch between different weapons and tools on the fly keeps gameplay dynamic, and the variety of enemy designs ensures that players must continually adapt their tactics. The inclusion of glitches as a gameplay mechanic adds an inventive twist, allowing players to manipulate the game world in ways that feel like hacking the system itself, fitting seamlessly into the game’s technological theme.  The narrative is cryptic yet compelling, slowly unraveling through environmental storytelling, cryptic logs, and intriguing characters encountered along the way. The story follows Trace, a scientist who wakes up in an alien world after a mysterious accident, piecing together the truth behind the strange landscape and his own identity. This approach to storytelling respects the player’s intelligence, offering fragments of lore that encourage theorizing and exploration rather than spoon-feeding every detail. The dark, atmospheric tone of the story complements the gameplay perfectly, creating an experience that is both intellectually engaging and emotionally resonant.  While Axiom Verge may initially appeal to fans of retro platformers, it transcends simple nostalgia by introducing fresh ideas and deep gameplay systems. The difficulty curve is well-balanced, offering enough challenge to satisfy veterans without alienating newcomers. However, some players might find the sheer size of the world and the abundance of secrets overwhelming at first, but this only adds to the game’s longevity and replayability. The map system and the gradual acquisition of new abilities ensure that progress feels rewarding and natural, maintaining a satisfying pace throughout.  In summary, Axiom Verge is a masterful blend of retro inspiration and modern design sensibilities, delivering a richly detailed, challenging, and atmospheric Metroidvania experience. Its captivating art style, innovative gameplay mechanics, and subtle narrative create a memorable journey that stands out in the indie game scene. Whether you’re a long-time fan of the genre or a newcomer curious about what Metroidvania games have to offer, Axiom Verge provides an engrossing adventure filled with mystery, exploration, and thrilling combat. It is a shining example of how passion and creativity can produce a game that feels both timeless and refreshingly original.  Rating: 8/10

Axiom Verge ★★★★★ (5/5)  Duração: 15h38  imgur.com/a/pLyNz0E  Jogo sensacional! Embora eu não estivesse muito na vibe de um metroidvania quando iniciei esse, ele conseguiu me conquistar. O jogo é belíssimo, a trilha sonora é muito marcante e os itens são muito bem balanceados, tanto é que ele não tem pulo duplo mesmo sendo um metroidvania e o jogo flui muito bem.  Contudo, eu tive muita sorte ao explorar esse jogo, porque eu, coincidentemente, segui pelo caminho que deveria ser seguido em quase todas as vezes. Durante todo o meu gameplay, acho que fiquei perdido apenas uma vez.  A história não é o grande forte do jogo, porque eu confesso que terminei sem entender muita coisa, mas esse não é um jogo de narrativa, é de exploração, e ele nos instiga a explorar de uma forma brilhante. Ele faz isso tão bem que o jogo não tem um mecanismo de viagem rápida, mas isso não faz falta. Embora o mapa seja grande e variado, você consegue, rapidamente, cruzar ele para explorar novamente áreas que você havia visitado previamente.  Para os fãs do gênero, esse título é um “must-play”.

This game does well in many things such as: Story, soundtrack, and fantastic pixel art when it comes to graphics. I was sure this game would be a masterpiece from the moment I started it, but it wasn't like that... after advancing a little and defeating the first boss, which was 'meh', I thought the next ones would be better.... As I progressed, I realized that the developers had failed at one of the most important things... boss fights... which are either too simple and annoying or just plain annoying.... even the final boss, who I hoped would be the best and cheer me up was weak. The game isn't perfect in terms of abilities, which sometimes don't work as they should... For me, this game could have been a 9/10 or more if the bosses had been executed properly, but in its current state, it's a 7.5/10 game. It's not a bad game overall, if you're a fan of metroidvania genre , you might be able to overlook these "problems".

🎮 Axiom Verge – Minha Experiência no Mundo Pixelado! 🎮  Olha, Axiom Verge me surpreendeu de um jeito muito positivo! O mapa é um prato cheio pra quem ama explorar cada cantinho e descobrir segredos escondidos — sério, tem muita coisa! Cada nova área parece um convite pra se perder (no bom sentido) e sair vasculhando tudo.  Se eu pudesse dar uma sugestão de leve pro jogo, seria: que tal um sistema de teletransporte entre os pontos de save? Isso daria uma baita força na hora de se locomover, principalmente quando você quer revisitar lugares com habilidades novas.  A história tem aquele mistério que te prende, com um toque sci-fi que combina perfeitamente com o estilo do jogo. E sim, o desafio é real! Conforme você vai evoluindo, a curva de dificuldade acompanha, e isso deixa tudo mais empolgante.  Me diverti muito jogando, e minha nota pessoal é um sólido 8/10! Já tô tão no clima que Axiom Verge 2 já tá garantido na minha lista. 😄

Por que o jogo é tão bom?   O jogo te joga em um mundo estranho e hostil (Sudra) sem muitas explicações. O mapa é enorme, interconectado e cheio de segredos. A progressão é orgânica: você encontra um obstáculo intransponível, explora em outra direção, adquire uma nova arma ou habilidade e, de repente, um caminho inteiro se abre. Essa sensação de descoberta é o coração de qualquer bom Metroidvania, e Axiom Verge executa isso com maestria.   Diferente de muitos jogos do gênero que possuem uma ou duas armas principais, Axiom Verge te dá um arsenal absurdo e criativo. São dezenas de armas, cada uma com um comportamento único. Desde um simples disparo frontal até um laser que ricocheteia nas paredes, um drone que você controla à distância e até uma arma que corrompe os inimigos, transformando-os em plataformas ou aliados temporários. Essa variedade mantém o combate sempre fresco e experimental.  As habilidades de mobilidade não são apenas para chegar a novos lugares; elas são integradas de forma inteligente aos quebra-cabeças e à exploração. A habilidade de "atravessar" paredes usando um drone, ou o Disruptor (um dispositivo que "glitcha" o ambiente), são mecânicas geniais que forçam você a pensar de forma não linear.

This is... mostly pretty great. It makes me wish the Steam voting system wasn't just "Yes/No" because it'd definitely not be that far yes.  It's a perfectly solid retro-styled metroidvania. Some fun music and vibes and some creative mechanics too. Exploring the world and stuff was pretty fun for at least the first 2/3rds of the game.  The last act gets a bit rough though. A few too many annoying to deal with or strong overworld enemies that are hard to hit without just tanking damage. Some bosses that are the same. Towards the end you've explored most of most areas so you end up retracing your steps lots and lots to find the one nook or cranny you might not have used your new ability on in case it unlocks a new area.   There's also a classic metroidvania issue of this huge abundance of different weapons you can use - most of which feel basically useless and like you may as well just use the base weapon or one or two others.   It's still pretty decent and fun, but to be blunt I can't say I'd pay full price for it if I knew (I think I got it on cheap though).

Reminiscent of the original Metroid, but much more complicated. There are a ton of weapons that function quite differently. The combat is mostly fun. Certain enemies I found to be quite annoying, with no simple way to deal with them. I'm sure there was a way, I just didn't know how. They just move fast and make it difficult to hit them while avoiding projectiles or the actual enemy. Most were easy enough to deal with. The bosses are all elaborate and cool looking. There are some equipment upgrades that make traversal and exploration easier and more interesting. There is one movement ability that is tied to the d-pad and it doesn't really work great, I didn't think so anyway. I know all the other buttons were tied up with other stuff, but it made it very difficult to use to get to some of the secrets and awkward in combat.  I actually played this game years ago, but had Axiom Verge 2 in my library for a while and finally decided to play it. Figured I'd give this game a run through again, as it only took me 12 hours the first time.  The game really needs fast travel. I get that they want you to explore, but revisiting old areas to get to an area where I missed a room got old. I know there is a way to sorta get around quickly, but you still have to get there first. I dunno, just seems pretty normal for this type of game, where things can't be accessed yet because you are missing an upgrade, to provide a way to move quickly between save points. I ended up not collecting everything when I got to the boss area because I didn't feel like walking there.  Anyway, great game, lots of fun. Cool story and not super challenging. The asking price is fair for what you get, but if it's on sale, I'd definitely pick it up.

Granting this game is a decade old, its retro style makes it a bit timeless, so I figure I'll drop a review anyway.  I wanted to give this game a longer try, though I was on the fence about it throughout my time with it. I do really like the visuals (I know), and soundtrack (I know), the gameplay seemed from my time getting just a little way past the first boss, passable and interesting, if challenging, though I worried it would become boring and frustrating before too much longer, as the 2nd boss was especially boring and repetitive, taking a very long time to defeat even after getting its pattern down and taking no damage the whole time. The platforming was also a little frustrating at points.  Ultimately what made me put it down however, is the aiming system. You have to hold one of the triggers to lock the character in place so you can use the movement controls to aim his weapon (d-pad or stick), as the other stick selects the weapon type. I think it would have been better if the right stick aimed, so movement could be uninterrupted, and you wouldn't have to hold down a shoulder button. Swapping weapon types was not, at least as far as I got, necessary to do at such a moments notice it needed the right stick dedicated to it at all times. This whole scheme made the controls difficult in an already fairly punishing game (enemies do a lot of damage). These controls actively hurt my hands after awhile (a chronic issue for me with certain games), meaning I could only play it for about an hour at a time before having to put it down, after three sessions of this I decided to give up. I'm quite disappointed as I would have liked to see where the game was going.

看了GMTK的推荐视频来的。地图设计是纯正的密特罗德味。如果喜欢密特罗德的话你也一定会喜欢Axiom Verge的 但是我玩不下去弃坑了……目前进度大概只到了流程的一半(?) 主要弃坑原因时玩不下去任何需要钩爪的游戏。超银的钩爪手感不错，但超银里需要钩爪的地方每个点也能卡我不少时间。这游戏的钩爪手感十分神秘，我完全无法掌握……刚拿到钩爪走回头路的地方卡了我十分钟:( 看起来后面探图仍然需要大量使用钩爪……也许以我的能力无法继续探索下去了，就到这里弃坑吧 视觉画面 - ★★★☆☆ 精细的类sfc画风完全戳在了我的好球区，一些机械装置的设计能看出作者深受吉格尔的影响w 但是部分地区墙壁的颜色搭配有点反直觉，配合上secret world的crt滤镜很多时候甚至会让我分不清前景层和背景层……在一些需要平台跳跃的地方初见还是挺折磨人的（完全没发现有些落脚点可以站上去） 音乐音效 - ★★★★☆ 符合游戏的整体风格，完全不会出戏 但是部分地区的音乐衔接有问题，BGM放完一遍后会停个两秒再重播。初见的时候听到音乐暂时停了吓一跳，以为要打boss了（） 玩法机制 - ★★★★☆ 主要玩法就是纯正的类密特罗德玩法——带有锁钥设计的地图探索配上作为调剂的战斗。很多能力都会给我一种意想不到的新奇感（看到只有一格宽的缝隙时虽然知道要用以后拿到的能力……但是完全没想到竟然是用这样的能力通过的） 可惜的是BOSS战难度似乎有点太简单了，完全没给我在玩超银时那种和BOSS苦战的感觉（也可能是因为我只进行到了游戏的中期，还没遇见太难的BOSS）。在死上个十多次（BOSS房门口就有存档点所以试错成本非常低）后就能完全摸清BOSS的机制，找到安定点后打BOSS比打小怪轻松多了 优化适配 - ★★★★★ 如果你使用的设备能流畅运行Steam客户端，并能在Steam的内置浏览器中看到我发的这条评测——那你在运行这个游戏时应该也不会出现性能方面的障碍 上手难度 - ★★☆☆☆ 近乎没有的引导（其实在地图设计上还是不错的，顺着路走几乎总能找到正路。但是游戏内并没有一个“你应该去哪里”的提示，就连“你应该往哪个方向前进”的提示都没有。很难说这是优点还是缺点）+较长的跑图流程可能会劝退一些现代玩家……只建议狂热的密特罗德粉丝游玩（比方说玩完超银后毫不觉得超银过时的）

This recommendation is fairly tepid, but it does enough things right in the end.  Main complaints:  - Usual metroidvania backtracking / getting lost (I probably had to google where to go about a dozen times).  - Several boss fights are unfair; they're just a check of "did you find this 'optional' weapon that makes the fight doable".  - Combat overall gets worse towards the end of the game.  Enemies are irritating and you'll probably end up avoiding them or using the old "shoot through the wall" strat to get by.  - Default controller settings are attrocious.  You'll find yourself accidentally switching weapons and dashing when you don't mean to (once you unlock the dash).    - Graphic choppiness/framerate was quite bad for me.  And the game uses a blinking "flinch" whenever you get hit that makes you lose track of your character.  - One zone has this ♥♥♥♥♥♥ wailing soundtrack and you somehow spend half the game in this ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ zone.  Good things:  - Interesting story, when it decides to show up.  - Decent enough metroidvania mechanics.  - Some cool graphics and setpieces.  - Gameplay is better until you're about 75% of the way through.

Yep, its Metroid.  Interesting power-ups. Sound design is horrendous. Loooooots of backtracking.  Enemies are very annoying. Seems like most of them just LUNGE at you at lightspeed and is basically a guarantened hit since you lack defensive moves. Some are too small to shoot, but as soon as you duck to shoot them, they lunge at you and you take a hit. There are enemies that keep jumping at you, without giving pause. Other enemies just seem to spam bullets everywhere, so you have to kill them before a stray bullet hits you. A very annoying type of enemy are the ones you need to corrupt first before being able to damage them.  Bosses on the other hands were too easy and predictable.  Has a very annoying map system. Unlike Metroid, it doesn't marks places with items and while there is a mark system, it only lets you place two marks per zone. Finding secret rooms is very annoying too. Each save file has 5 rooms in different spots, and if you didn't notice the scanlines effect from getting near them, good luck going back to every other possible entrance trying to find it. Also there are no warp rooms, so all backtracking is done MANUALLY.  Has a lot of different weapons, and I can only ask: why? At first you get weapons to reach a few buttons, but then you stop needing them right after. Most have weird bullet patterns that don't help in most situations. Once you get the ice machine gun and the flamethrower, I don't see any reason to use any other weapon. Its not like some enemies are vulnerable to certain weapons like the missiles from Metroid, so why bother? I was baffled that the last weapon you get is an useless boomerang gun.

This game was amazing! One of my very few favorite games of all time! The story and plot was truly unique. The game play is just as good; being able to collect numerous different weapons and tools while also collecting secret notes. There's also SO MANY secrets! Make sure to use all your tools. The enemies are also very cool. There definitely are a few which I DESPISE, purely because they're annoying, but that's what makes it fun. The soundtrack is also AMAZING! Whenever I get to a new area, I pause for a moment to just enjoy the music. I would honestly listen to this soundtrack in my free time. I also enjoy how it's not totally impossible. Like some boss fights are definitely difficult but they're not something that I would spend hours on. Each boss has there own kind of way to attack and I really enjoy that. It's also very nice how beating the game doesn't take 50+ hours(which doesn't automatically make a game bad, it's just nice to not spend forever playing the same game). I would definitely recommend this game to anyone, especially if you're into 2D games, which I 100% am. I know some people can't stand 2D games and tend to lean more towards 3D ones, but I'd still recommend this game. I had a lot of fun playing this and it will definitely be inbeded in my brain forever.

[h2]Axiom Verge is a decent enough Metroidvania, but there are many others that are more worth your time[/h2]  If I could give this game a thumbs sideways I would--[b]It has some interesting mechanics and refreshing progression but is ultimately just a little 'meh'[/b]. You can tell from the screenshots/trailers that Axiom Verge is a love letter to Metroid series and a more retro style of gaming in general and it achieves this nicely while still injecting a cool modern flare, but for me the game was too simple and old school:[list][*]Aesthetically unpleasing art style--clunky pixel art filled with an abrasive colour pallet[*]Annoying 8-bit repetitive techno--some people LOVE the sound track though[*]Mechanically bare-bones enemy and boss fights--Shoot, shoot, move out of the way...that's it[*]Janky hitboxes[*]Unforgiving platforming[*]Even though most areas do eventually loop round into each other, the movement still feels arduous and the lack of fast travel is very apparent[/list] [b](If it's the retro send-up that has attracted you to this game then take all this with a pinch of my salt):[/b]  What Axiom Verge does really well though, is surprising you with its unique upgrades and abilities. [b]It subverts the usual Mvania progression patterns[/b]--figuring out what you can do with your incrementallly increasing arsenal is fun and nails what Mvania's should primarily set out to do.  Despite this, and with the at-best serviceable story, my overall impression after finishing was "Yeah, that was okay". [b]I didn't not enjoy it, Its just isn't going to crop up in my mind when I think of great Metroidvanias[/b]

Axiom Verge, from what I've gathered, is a homage to the genre of metroidvania. At first I was very intrigued by the specific art style and felt like this could be an incredible adventure. However, I've tried getting into the game and its narrative on 3 separate occasions and can tell you that I don't want to see any more. I've had the feeling while playing that something was off and checked the other reviews, especially the ones that had constructive positive and negative criticism and found myself resonancing with others on a couple of issues the game has displayed to me so far.  Firstly, I think that the game fails on the gameplay aspect a lot. The movement and general 8 directional shooting style feels very clunky. I think that has to do with the overall level design though because the game keeps trying to overwhelm you with enemies all over the place where it becomes irritating not being able to control your shooting more freely. Even though as I've said it tries to pay a homage to those old school games that used to have that kind of gameplay mechanics, it fails on delivering it in a modern compact form.  Secondly, the overall level design feels boring. I've played this game for 2 hours and I can't tell you anything about even one area that stuck with me. Most of the time I was just walking through big spacious rooms with no meaning, a bunch of enemies and only one goal of getting ASAP to the next room. I'm not even sure how maintainable backtracking is if the game will go on for another 6-10 hours like this. Also, the rooms do have hidden interactions built into them, but because of the poor communication towards the player I've ended up missing most of them and was surprised when something randomly happened and I was like oh that's possible?  Lastly, I've had a lack of interest for the protagonist and the setting. This is the last point because I feel like that with more time this would be less of a complaint but I feel like the delivery of the story failed on me. Not being able to characterize or give background for our main character and therefore giving a lack of motivation means there is no reason to connect at least on that front to see what will happen to him.  If the core gameplay loop would've been different, I could ignore the other problems the game has in my opinion but combining all three of those leads me to the conclusion of not wanting to see more of this.  In conclusion, I don't recommend playing this. I do appreciate the art style though.

A great game all things considered, and though there are some gripes here and there, I can definitely recommend it to anyone who enjoys Metroidvanias. It's stunning it was created by one person alone, more than a decade ago at this point   The art really is beautiful, capturing the aesthetic and atmosphere of old NES / SNES games really well while putting it's own touch on it. Few modern games with a pixel art style take inspiration from this era so this is really refreshing to see. The limited colour pallets with with high contrasts, lots of darker areas and careful use of saturation are combined with really well refined technique make for art at the ceiling of this medium.  The music is solid all the way through with some bangers sprinkled throughout  The gameplay is also great and really enjoyable for the most part. A majority of the tools for progression you find throughout the game are unique to this game and were fun to use and experiment with. As someone with a lot of experience in games, it still took me some time to figure out some applications of all the tools and how to solve some of the puzzles which has become mostly trivial for me nowadays. Combat and exploration was also great with all the typical elements you expect from Metroidvanias, like being able to revisit previous areas and finding lots of new things. Though the bossfights definitely weren't this games strong suit with a lot of them being either frustrating, not challenging at all or both at once. There were also some quite annoying rooms, mostly in the early game but the game is pretty generous with checkpoints so they weren't that big of an issue all things considered. Some of the abilities cannot be controlled all that well which is probably one of the greater flaws of this game.  Lastly in regards to the story, I didn't have any expectations but was more than pleasantly surprised. The story is pretty neat having great twists and elements you don't really find anywhere else. It does also fit well with and enhance the overall atmosphere quite a bit  Though there some points at which you can lose your way throughout, it still is a great game. Maybe it's not the best for people who are entirely new to the genre or this type of atmosphere but if you found some the points mentioned intriguing, you should definitely give the game a try

This entire game can be summed up in the phrase "Quantity over quality".  New weapon you just found? It doesn't really feel like an upgrade, it just feels meh. Unless it was the Flamethrower, but that really is about it.  New tool to help with progression? 95% of the stuff you find with it feel more "meh". It's stuff, but it isn't good for much.  The game also has several stupidly cryptic puzzles that you don't even get given any real point in the right direction in how you are supposed to solve. 95% of players will just use a guide, as there is ZERO nudge in the right direction of what you should do, and one even requires the use of tools outside of the game to solve. No hints are given in this either. Only the most obsessive fanboy with no life will figure it out without a guide, and even if you do figure it out without a guide.. your reward is more "meh".  There is a very crappy and lazily made hub zone smack in the middle of all the rest of the zones, that only gives you a head to float on and take you through this very long tube for fast travel. The head isn't even that fast. This hub offers the most underwhelming quality of life in traversing some VERY BIG maps. Putting a teleport option in save rooms would have quickly made the big map slog massively more bearable. On top of this there is no really good method of quickly hurling yourself through 95% of the maps as they aren't all left to right tubes where drone launching for fast travel would have been useful.  The story line was also stupidly cryptic, and even the ending doesn't feel like really an ending at all. Just more unanswered questions. This was 18 hours I feel robbed of. Save your money and get a different metroid styled game. Better yet go play the old games, they did a lot more with a hell of a lot less.  I will also recommend reading some of the other negative reviews that elaborate more on some of the things I didn't. There is far more gripes with this mediocre game then just the ones I listed.

75/100.  Un metroidvania con muy buenas ideas y una ambientación cyberpunk muy lograda en pixel art. Una gran cantidad de armas y secretos por descubrir, ya que tendremos que volver sobre nuestros pasos para utilizar nuestras nuevas habilidades y escavar los recovecos en cada escenario con alto nivel de exploración. Su profunda historia depende de la obtención de documentos resguardados en lugares a descubrir, lo que lastra un poco y obliga al jugador a dedicarle más de lo necesario. Se deja muchas cosas por el camino y no es tan redondo como parece, sin embargo, es un título muy entretenido. Recomendado si tenéis amor por el género y paciencia.   A metroidvania with very good ideas and a well-executed cyberpunk setting in pixel art. A large number of weapons and secrets to discover, as we'll have to retrace our steps to use our new abilities and dig into the nooks and crannies of each highly explorable setting. Its deep story depends on obtaining documents hidden in undiscovered locations, which is a bit of a drag and forces the player to dedicate more time than necessary. It leaves many things behind and isn't as well-rounded as it seems; however, it's a very entertaining title. Recommended if you have a love for the genre and patience.

An excellent metroidvania that aims to sit alongside the original Metroid on the NES. Weapon selection is great with interesting weird science contraptions. Combat is a good balance, not too easy nor too difficult. Exploration is solid, where areas open up over time with upgrades. Story is interesting, but never in the way of the game-play. Music and sound are rough though, mimic of the classics too faithfully. Extended play sessions can hurt the ears. Axiom Verge is a fairly standard metroidvania that wears its Metroid influences on its sleeve. It came out in 2015, before the big post-Hollow Knight trend of inserting souls-like mechanics into the genre, so people who do not fancy those can play the game without the fear of corpse runs, or super challenging fights. However, it also lacks some more modern mechanics, such as fast-travel, or a more robust map marker system.  Combat is based on shooting and not melee. Throughout the game you will find plenty of different weapons: some really powerful, others useful in niche situations, and a couple of duds which seem more like meme choices. The game starts challenging, but over the course of the playthrough, it becomes increasingly easy, in part thanks to damage and health upgrades you can find almost everywhere, but also in part of rather simplistic enemy designs and combat patterns.  Exploration is where the game shines IMO. The world is weird and interesting to explore (helped by a great soundtrack), and that exploration is aided by an arsenal of fairly unique abilities, the most interesting of which is the Adress Disruptor: a hacking gun which can be used to change the behavior of enemies. The traversal itself, however, is rather slow, as you do not get many zippy movement abilities which could allow you to just rush past enemies.  Overall I'd rate the game 8/10, but with a caveat that I really really REALLY loved the setting, the overall aesthetic and tone of the game, and especially the music. If these don't grab you in the same way, feel free to knock the rating down a notch.

Very solid game. I can see this being a divisive Metroidvania, because it's designed to be much, much more of a Metroid game than the standard more mixed, balanced formula. I enjoyed this, but I grew up on Matroid, spending hundreds and hundreds of hours in Metroid 2 alone, so it may not be to everyone's taste.  There's a wide variety of different weapons here, consistent upgrades, some very well-hidden secrets--many of which I actually felt really joyed to find, I felt accomplished actually having the intuition to seek some of these out... but it can be tough. I've beaten the game with a near 90% completion for map and items, and I'm missing several of the main health and weapon upgrades and at least one weapon... this last I found, but it seems to be sealed away beyond my ability to get to, and seeing as how I have all of the traversal abilities unlocked I can only assume it's either accessed from somewhere else on the map I haven't discovered yet or through some other means.  That aside, the difficulty can be pretty tough from time to time, but as you get upgrades and find a weapon you can get used to, it starts to click. Some of the enemies can be ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, some of the weapons are more fun than they are useful, and the lack of a late game quick travel is more slightly annoying than actually troublesome; most of what I wanted it for was late-stage item hunting, but it is what it is.  That said, the game has a wealth of interesting lore, a very odd high-concept world with strange entities and narrative twists and turns really related to the environment and your existence in it. There's also a whole hoard of interesting ideas implemented in the game, as well; odd 'glitches' as part of your movement and exploration, as well as your combat, diegetic death and rebirth, and a note and password system you should NOT ignore to get the most out of the game.  I can recommend this easily, and I'm extremely excited to play 2 when I can, but don't take the association with Metroid lightly. This is a very distinct Metroidvania with much, much more of the Metroid in the mix and if you aren't a fan of those core concepts, you might struggle to find value here for yourself.

プレイ時間12時間弱で、ノーマルモードクリア。  なかなかに濃厚なメトロイド風ゲームだった。  メトロイドや悪魔城ドラキュラなどのメトロイドヴァニアは結構遊んでいて、steam上で評判が良かったのと、スーファミ風グラフィックにひかれて購入。  最初の数時間くらいは、あまり質の良くないゲームかな…と思った。一瞬、やめようかなとも思った。  ストーリーは何が言いたいのかよくわからないし、キャラも警戒に画面を動けるというわけではなく、やっていて爽快感よりストレスのほうがたまる。出てくるボスも何回も死亡するくらい強いし。  ただ、我慢してやっているうちにキャラクターの移動などは、パワーアップによってどんどん快適になっていき、ボスの強さなども、初見だとかなり強く感じるものの、やっているうちにパターンや攻略法が見えてきて、きちんと倒せるようになっている。そのあたりは、ちゃんと計算して作られていることを感じた。  難易度はメトロイドシリーズなどより、ワンランク上くらい高い印象を受けるものの、理不尽すぎるということはなく、必要な武器などをしっかり回収して、ボスのパターンも見極めれば、十分にクリアできる範囲。  自分は途中で一回、ゲームを投げかけているので、知り合いに勧めるかと聞かれたら、ちょっと悩むところではあるが、レトロなメトロイドヴァニアが好きな人なら、十分楽しめるゲームではないかと思う。  ちなみにこのゲームは、リモコンドローンでの移動や壁抜けなど、本家メトロイドにはない面白いアイデアが満載で、ゲームをやらないまでも、プレイ動画でそれらのギミックを見ているだけでも楽しめるかもしれない。

Overall a pretty solid Metroid clone. It's a chill experience to go around seeking powerups and exercising your navigational skills, blasting biotechnological monsters on the way. It wasn't engrossing but it was fun enough to complete while listening to podcasts over the course of a few days. Doesn't feel like a titan of the genre as it may have once been.  The general exploration is probably the highlight. It's densely filled with powerups, and the winding path I took to collect all the upgrades flows pretty well. It's got a typical amount of backtracking, but the game world isn't too big, and it's pretty common that you run across something of interest while doing it. Also, if something is hidden really well, it's usually optional, so I rarely got stuck looking for the next thing to do.  As far as the movement itself goes, I'm slightly mixed. At the start I found it perfectly smooth, and there's one upgrade at the end that I really like. However, the double-tap dash is difficult and taxing to use a lot (plus you generally can't hold the attack button to fire, you have to mash it), and near the end you're expected to sequence it with other abilities in ways that felt unpleasant and awkward to do. I found the grapple a bit awkward too. Pretty standard Metroid fare.  The combat was not fun. Most enemies would do the same attack over and over, and with plentiful cover, I found the best strategy was usually to stand in one place and jump and shoot periodically. This was especially true of bosses; I would find cover and rinse-repeat them until I won. When there was no cover, or I couldn't be bothered to be careful, I found I could often just ignore the bullets and go all-out on aggression until an enemy or boss died and live to tell the tale. Not awful gameplay, but not appealing either.  The story and atmosphere was okay. I liked what the plot was going for overall. It's got horror elements, but the pace of the gameplay and plot is so quick that the tone didn't really sink in for me, leaving the aesthetic feeling a little hollow. Also, although the art did a good job, info on the zones and their histories and purposes was a bit too scarce for me to care much about the setting. For some, though, I'm sure the game's specific horror-tinged biotech aesthetic is so appealing that they'll feel differently.

this game has a.. vibe. if you easily get nauseous from distorted visuals like me, it might take a little time to get used to the graphics, BUT, power it through. once you get used to it, the game is absolutely AMAZING. things I really like about this game are: - Rusalki.. the reason that made me immediately buy this game was Elsenova in the cover image of the game. exploring the lore via interactions with these entities gave me euphoria every time. and oh boy, [spoiler] the stage where we finally get to see their bodies [/spoiler] made me stare at the screen for half an hour. - the music! though the creator claims that the music was the easiest to make, he did a brilliant job enhancing the environment with the banger soundtrack. - upgrades. every time when you think that you figured out the next upgrade to progress, the game slaps you and gives you a completely unusual item that you would ever expect, yet makes perfect sense.  shortly, the game worths every penny. also, after playing the game, I suggest watching the documentary of how the game was made, as well as some of the speedrun commentaries by the creator to further appreciate the genius of Thomas Happ.

Axiom Verge is one of those retro inspired games that thinks retro means slow, tedious, unattractive and unaccommodating.   It starts of well enough. The controls are tight and you get a couple of nice weapons and power ups to intrigue you but the more you progress the more the game unravels. Rooms become too big for how slow the movement is. They're stuffed with unavoidable and increasingly cheap enemies that, on occasion, will automatically hit you on entrance. Given that there's no fast travel this makes backtracking a real chore.   Part of what makes Metroid and Metroidvania's great is the discovery loop. It's the reward for exploration that opens up exploration that leads to more rewards. In Axiom Verge it felt like you were chasing some invisible carrot and every time you managed to catch it that carrot was miniscule and spoiled. Every power up didn't make you feel any more powerful it felt like a bare minimum necessity for dealing with the next room's spongy enemies. Most weapons felt pointless outside of some very specific situation. The analog for morph ball, the drone you control remotely, is a poor substitute whose long deactivation animation feels too punishing for failing to throw it where you intended. This game's version of jump range increase didn't feel like the proper map opening bit of freedom like it should in these sorts of games. The grappling hook just felt awkward and finicky.  The game's two best items - the drill and the hacking tool - are unlocked early in the game but their utilization doesn't extend too much beyond the initial novelty. Which is the issue, instead of focusing on less powers and honing them and scenarios for them to be as fun as possible Axiom Verge goes for the quantity over quality approach. The soundtrack is really good, though.

Приятная метроидвания, но отвратительный платформер.   Главная проблема в новых способностях, которые дают по мере прохождения. Например, управление крюком настолько неинтуитивно, что вы будете часто падать в пропасти. Управление неплавное, отзывчивость никакая.  Но апогей всего безобразия с платформингом — это местный аналог деша/телепорта. Его просто неудобно использовать как минимум на Steam Deck что на стике, что на крестовине. Способность активируется двойным нажатием, но дальность короткая, направление в полёте выбирать почти невозможно. А теперь представьте, что разработчики усложнили это дизайном локаций.  Я в какой-то сюжетный момент просто застрял, так как деш буквально не добивал до платформы. Хотя это изначально подразумевалось (просмотрел множество прохождений). В итоге пришлось биться головой о косяк разработчиков около сотню попыток и тратить время на ерунду. В других местах сделали приемлемо.  В первый раз с таким сталкивался. Хотя играл в Hollow Knight, The Messenger, Shovel Knight и прочее.

Axiom Verge was an enjoyable game! As far as 2D side scrolling metroidvania types of games go, this one definitely hit on all the retro nostalgia that I was looking for, and it had a cool little story to boot! My only gripe with the game was the necessity of double tapping the joystick and/or D-pad later on in the game in order to use the pretty necessary dash mechanic. It felt terrible. It was wonky, unresponsive, and you'd end up using it many times by mistake. Overall it broke the gameflow, broke immersion, and put a slight damper on the overall experience. With that being said though, despite the controls driving me crazy at times, the positives FAR outweigh the negatives. I'd recommend this game to anyone looking to scratch that 2D side-scroller itch. Overall it was still a very enjoyable experience and worth the buy!

Just trying this out here in 2025.  Perfect steam deck game.  Works like a champ.  I did remap the button controls for myself, so that is something this game offers which is great.  I primarily wanted to play Axiom Verge 2, but since there was a package deal and this one was really cheap, I decided why not start from the beginning.  This is a fun little Metroidvania.  Not super complicated, and as of yet not super challenging.  I have 2 bosses down, both were fairly simple to figure out.  There are some tougher enemies that jump around quite a bit, so you do have to be on your toes.  The map is decent enough, if perhaps a bit small on the screen but you can see the doors and such if you look closely.  Map markers are pretty limited... you can only drop them on the spot you are standing, and you can also only drop two types.  A numeral 1 or a numeral 2.  So you kinda have to figure out your own scheme for that. And make no mistake, this is a Metroidvania in the sense that you will backtrack quite a bit, and you have to find the specific pathway that takes you to the next spot you need to go.  There are no quest markers or anything, so it's up to you to explore and map the region out.  I haven't had any problems so far with that, but sometimes the backtracking does cost you time, so be aware.  But if you play Metroidvania's regularly, this should be a familiar concept.  My only gripe is that this is one of those games where they have tuned some of the platforming to be right at the limit of your jump, which means you have to really focus on your jump timing sometimes.  Not all the time, but it does come up.  And if you miss the jump, usually you fall down where an enemy is so you can take damage that way.  But I mean to be fair, that's a small gripe.  Overall, this is one of the simpler examples (so far) of the genre, but that isn't to say there's anything negative.  Everything about it is super retro, including the 8-bit synth music that changes depending on the biome you are in.  I don't have a full grip on the story yet, but it is definitely intriguing.  I've unlocked 3 weapons (or really 3 styles of attack from one weapon.. details), and each of them is quite different, though they do analogue to familiar concepts.  You have the fast firing, low damage one (rifle?), the slow firing, high damage one that can also be triggered to explode (rocket launcher), and the short range burst one (shotgun).  There's probably lots more.  There also appear to be a bunch of different unlocks and things for the character that you get.  Definitely seems very cool, and from what I can tell from the videos on the steam page, it should ramp up the difficulty a bit in general so maybe it's just taking it easy on me as I'm starting out?  Dunno, have to keep playing.  Anyway don't shy away from this one just because it's older, it's actually pretty fun.  And from what I understand, it is also backstory to Axiom Verge 2 which is a more functionally appealing game.

This is basically a Metroid game under another name. Most Metroidvanias are more Vania then Metroid, so this was a welcome change!   There are a ton of different weapons and upgrades and secrets to find, and just exploring the world is a bunch of fun.   However, I do think it's difficult to know where to go next often at all and the map doesn't really help. I would often think that an area of the map that I hadn't fully explored yet would be unlocked with something I just picked up, then go across the whole map to it, only to realize it was a different barrier in place. I ended up restoring to guides at times just not to waste time on traversal.   A lot of the boss fights are inventive and fun, but I also felt like I had to cheese many of them to win by finding a strategy that seemed to trick the way they moved or attacked. This was kind of interesting, but felt a bit bad at times.   Overall had a great time though and will play the sequel soon.
